series.title: kho: history: track previous kernel version and kexec boot count
series.author: Breno Leitao <leitao@debian.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v9 0/6] kho: history: track previous kernel version and kexec boot count
[PATCH v9 1/6] kho: add size parameter to kho_add_subtree()
[PATCH v9 2/6] kho: rename fdt parameter to blob in kho_add/remove_subtree()
[PATCH v9 3/6] kho: persist blob size in KHO FDT
[PATCH v9 4/6] kho: fix kho_in_debugfs_init() to handle non-FDT blobs
[PATCH v9 5/6] kho: kexec-metadata: track previous kernel chain
[PATCH v9 6/6] kho: document kexec-metadata tracking feature

review.summary: The series generalizes KHO's subtree API to carry arbitrary blobs (not just FDTs) by adding an explicit size parameter and persisting it in the root FDT, then uses that to pass the previous kernel's version string and kexec boot count across kexec for early-boot diagnostics.
review.review_inline:
Breno Leitao <leitao@debian.org>

kho: history: track previous kernel version and kexec boot count

Patches 1-4 look correct. kho_add_subtree() locking is unchanged
(guard(mutex)(&kho_out.lock) still protects all FDT mutations), all
callers that previously ignored the size are updated to pass NULL for
the new optional size output in kho_retrieve_subtree(), the version
bump to kho-v3 is appropriate since blob-size is now a required
property, and kho_populate()'s strict compatible check guarantees a
v2 outgoing FDT is rejected before any subtree parsing occurs.

> diff --git a/kernel/liveupdate/kexec_handover.c b/kernel/liveupdate/kexec_handover.c

[ ... ]

> +	u64 size_u64 = size;
>  	err = fdt_setprop(root_fdt, off, KHO_SUB_TREE_PROP_NAME,
>  			  &phys, sizeof(phys));
>  	if (err < 0)
>  		goto out_pack;
> +
> +	err = fdt_setprop(root_fdt, off, KHO_SUB_TREE_SIZE_PROP_NAME,
> +			  &size_u64, sizeof(size_u64));
> +	if (err < 0)
> +		goto out_pack;

If the second fdt_setprop fails, fdt_pack() is called on a node that
has preserved-data but no blob-size.  All existing callers abort
kexec preparation on any non-zero return, so the partial node will
not be consumed in practice.  This was already the case before this
patch (a failed fdt_setprop for preserved-data left an empty node),
but there is now one more failure mode.  Would it be worth calling
fdt_del_node() before the fdt_pack() in the error path to keep the
root FDT clean?

> diff --git a/kernel/liveupdate/kexec_handover_debugfs.c b/kernel/liveupdate/kexec_handover_debugfs.c

[ ... ]

> +		blob_size = fdt_getprop(fdt, child,
> +					KHO_SUB_TREE_SIZE_PROP_NAME, &len);
> +		if (!blob_size || len != sizeof(*blob_size)) {
> +			pr_warn("node %s missing or invalid %s property\n",
> +				name, KHO_SUB_TREE_SIZE_PROP_NAME);
> +			continue;
> +		}

The continue on missing blob-size is correct: the kho-v3 compatible
check in kho_populate() guarantees that a v2 FDT (which lacks
blob-size) is already rejected before this loop runs, so the
continue path only fires for a corrupted v3 FDT and does not
silently drop legitimate entries.  Verified.

> diff --git a/include/linux/kho/abi/kexec_metadata.h b/include/linux/kho/abi/kexec_metadata.h

[ ... ]

> +struct kho_kexec_metadata {
> +	u32 version;
> +	char previous_release[__NEW_UTS_LEN + 1];
> +	u32 kexec_count;
> +} __packed;

With __packed, kexec_count sits at byte offset 69 (unaligned u32).
The compiler generates correct byte-wise access for __packed struct
members accessed through the struct pointer, so this is safe.
Please confirm the restore path does not extract a bare u32 * to
kexec_count and dereference it outside the packed struct context,
which would bypass the packed alignment handling.

The kexec_handover.c diff for patch 5 was truncated in the
submission, so the restore and prepare implementations are not
visible.  A few things to confirm in that code:

1. Does the restore path validate BOTH the blob size returned by
   kho_retrieve_subtree() and md->version before accessing any
   other struct fields?  A size check catches struct layout changes
   that happen to share the same version number; a version check
   alone is not sufficient.

2. Is kho_restore_free() called in all error paths as well as the
   success path?  kho_alloc_preserve() pins pages in the preserved
   memory set; skipping kho_restore_free() on a version or size
   check failure would permanently leak those pages to the new
   kernel's allocator.

3. What initcall level invokes kho_kexec_metadata_restore()?  The
   example output shows timestamp 0.000000, consistent with
   early_initcall.  kho_retrieve_subtree() requires kho_in.fdt_phys
   to be set by kho_populate(), which completes before
   early_initcall.  Please confirm this ordering is guaranteed.

patches.title: [PATCH v2] kernel/fork: validate exit_signal in kernel_clone()

review.summary: This patch adds a valid_signal() guard in kernel_clone() to reject exit_signal values outside [0, _NSIG], closing a syzkaller-reported path where clone() with flags=0x80 produced exit_signal=128 and triggered a WARN in do_notify_parent(). The fix is logically correct for all callers, but a stale in-function comment is left behind.
review.review_inline:
Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com> writes:

[PATCH v2] kernel/fork: validate exit_signal in kernel_clone()

The motivation is clear and the fix looks correct. I traced all
callers and checked valid_signal() semantics before forming this
opinion.

valid_signal() is defined as:

  static inline int valid_signal(unsigned long sig)
  {
          return sig <= _NSIG ? 1 : 0;
  }

The unsigned comparison means exit_signal=0 (the common case for
kernel threads spawned via kernel_thread() and user_mode_thread())
correctly passes the check.  SIGCHLD=17 used by fork/vfork also
passes.  Values 65-255, reachable through (clone_flags & CSIGNAL)
from a hostile clone() call, are correctly rejected with -EINVAL.

I checked every direct caller of kernel_clone():
  - kernel_thread() / user_mode_thread(): exit_signal=(flags &
    CSIGNAL), typically 0.  No regression.
  - sys_fork() / sys_vfork(): exit_signal=SIGCHLD.  No regression.
  - sys_clone() and all arch variants (ia32, sparc, m68k, nios2):
    exit_signal=(lower_32_bits(clone_flags) & CSIGNAL).  The
    invalid range 65-255 is now rejected, which is the intent.
  - sys_clone3(): copy_clone_args_from_user() already validates
    exit_signal with the same valid_signal() call, so the new
    check is a no-op for that path but harmless.

> diff --git a/kernel/fork.c b/kernel/fork.c
> @@ -2687,6 +2687,8 @@ pid_t kernel_clone(struct kernel_clone_args *args)
>  	    (args->pidfd == args->parent_tid))
>  		return -EINVAL;
>  
> +	if (!valid_signal(args->exit_signal))
> +		return -EINVAL;
>  	/*
>  	 * Determine whether and which event to report to ptracer.  When

One minor nit: the comment in the kernel_clone() function header
(just above this function, not quoted here) still reads:

  * args->exit_signal is expected to be checked for sanity by the
  * caller.

That comment is now stale -- kernel_clone() enforces the check
itself.  Could you update it to reflect the new reality, so future
callers do not feel obligated to duplicate the validation?

series.title: mm/userfaultfd: re-validate vma in mfill_atomic() loop under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK
series.author: Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com>

patches.title: [PATCH] mm/userfaultfd: re-validate vma in mfill_atomic() loop under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK

review.summary: Adds a per-iteration VMA bounds check in mfill_atomic() to detect and recover from concurrent VMA splits under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK, where a racing UFFDIO_UNREGISTER can shrink vma->vm_end past the current dst_addr via a race window in vma_start_read().
review.review_inline:
On Mon, Mar 16 2026, Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com> wrote:

mm/userfaultfd: re-validate vma in mfill_atomic() loop under
CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK

I traced the reported race and the fix looks directionally correct.
Notes below.

> diff --git a/mm/userfaultfd.c b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> --- a/mm/userfaultfd.c
> +++ b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> @@ -910,6 +910,22 @@ static __always_inline ssize_t mfill_atomic(...)
>
>  	while (state.src_addr < src_start + len) {
>  		VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(state.dst_addr >= dst_start + len);
> +	       /*
> +		* Under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK, a concurrent UFFDIO_UNREGISTER

The comment opener uses a tab then seven spaces rather than a plain
tab, giving misaligned indentation.  checkpatch will flag this.

> +		if (state.dst_addr < state.vma->vm_start ||
> +		    state.dst_addr >= state.vma->vm_end) {
> +			mfill_put_vma(&state);
> +			state.dst_start = state.dst_addr;
> +			state.len = dst_start + len - state.dst_addr;
> +			err = mfill_get_vma(&state);
> +			if (err)
> +				break;
> +		}

I verified the resource management: mfill_put_vma() guards against a
NULL vma, so the mfill_put_vma() at line 938 is a no-op when
mfill_get_vma() fails here.  No double-release.

I confirmed that state.dst_start and state.len are exactly what
mfill_get_vma() passes to uffd_mfill_lock().  Setting dst_start =
dst_addr and len = (dst_start + len - dst_addr) is correct:
uffd_mfill_lock() under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK finds the VMA at the new
position and validates the remaining range fits within it.  If the
remaining range spans VMAs (e.g. multiple concurrent splits),
mfill_get_vma() returns -ENOENT, the loop breaks, and the caller sees
partial progress.  Userspace can retry with the outstanding range,
which is the existing contract.

I also checked mfill_copy_folio_retry(): it calls mfill_put_vma() and
mfill_get_vma() on the same state fields.  If this re-lookup already
fired on a prior iteration, the adjusted state.dst_start and state.len
are reused correctly by a subsequent copy retry, because dst_addr has
not advanced between the re-lookup and the retry.

One scope concern: the commit message and the comment both say "Under
CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK" but the new block is not wrapped in
#ifdef CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK.  Under the non-PER_VMA_LOCK path,
uffd_mfill_lock() holds mmap_read_lock() for the entire loop duration,
preventing concurrent VMA splits, so state.dst_addr >=
state.vma->vm_end can never be true there.  The check is dead code on
that path.  Would you consider wrapping the block in
#ifdef CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK ... #endif to match the stated intent and
avoid the per-iteration overhead on the mmap-lock path?

patches.title: [PATCH mm-unstable] mm/memcontrol: batch memcg charging in __memcg_slab_post_alloc_hook

review.summary: Batch the per-object memcg charging in __memcg_slab_post_alloc_hook() by scanning ahead for a contiguous run of objects whose slabs share the same pgdat, then issuing a single charge/consume call for the whole run; the per-object obj_ext assignment loop is preserved as-is.

patches.title: [PATCH] Docs: mmap_prepare: don't italicize structs

review.summary: Removes single backticks around 'struct page' references in mmap_prepare.rst so that kernel-doc automarkup renders them as bold monospace code style instead of italic HTML output.
review.review_inline:
Randy Dunlap <rdunlap@infradead.org>

Docs: mmap_prepare: don't italicize structs

Documentation-only change. Verified the two affected lines in
Documentation/filesystems/mmap_prepare.rst match the patch context
exactly. Removing the single backticks lets kernel-doc automarkup
detect the "struct xyz" pattern and render it as bold monospace rather
than italic HTML. No code is touched.

> -* mmap_action_map_kernel_pages() - Maps a specified array of `struct page`
> +* mmap_action_map_kernel_pages() - Maps a specified array of struct page
>    pointers in the VMA from a specific offset.
>
> -* mmap_action_map_kernel_pages_full() - Maps a specified array of `struct
> -  page` pointers over the entire VMA. The caller must ensure there are
> +* mmap_action_map_kernel_pages_full() - Maps a specified array of struct
> +  page pointers over the entire VMA. The caller must ensure there are

Both hunks are consistent with each other and with the surrounding
style in the file. Looks good to me.

series.title: selftests/mm: skip several tests when thp is not available
series.author: Chunyu Hu <chuhu@redhat.com>

patches.title: [PATCH v2 0/5] selftests/mm: skip several tests when thp is not available
[PATCH v2 1/5] selftests/mm/guard-regions: skip collapse test when thp not enabled
[PATCH v2 2/5] selftests/mm: soft-dirty: skip two tests when thp is not available
[PATCH v2 3/5] selftests/mm: move write_file helper to vm_util

[PATCH v2 5/5] selftests/mm: transhuge_stress: skip the test when thp not available

review.summary: This series adds thp_is_enabled() guard checks to five mm selftests so they skip cleanly instead of failing on kernels built without THP support (e.g. RT kernels). Patch 3 consolidates the duplicated write_file() helper into vm_util to eliminate a header conflict.

series.title: Migrate on fault for device pages
series.author: mpenttil@redhat.com

patches.title: [PATCH v6 0/6] Migrate on fault for device pages
[PATCH v6 1/6] mm:/Kconfig changes for migrate on fault for device pages
[PATCH v6 2/6] mm: Add helper to convert HMM pfn to migrate pfn
[PATCH v6 3/6] mm/hmm: do the plumbing for HMM to participate in migration
[PATCH v6 4/6] mm: setup device page migration in HMM pagewalk
[PATCH v6 5/6] mm: add new testcase for the migrate on fault case
[PATCH v6 6/6] mm:/migrate_device.c: remove migrate_vma_collect_*()

review.summary: This series unifies the HMM range-fault and migrate_vma page-table walks so a single walk can fault-in missing pages and install migration entries simultaneously. Several concrete bugs are present: a misplaced enum and inline function definition that prevents compilation under !CONFIG_MIGRATION, and multiple test-code defects that would silently produce zero migrations.

series.title: userfaultfd: unassigned vma leads to a potential unreleased locks
series.author: Edward Adam Davis <eadavis@qq.com>

patches.title: [PATCH next] userfaultfd: unassigned vma leads to a potential unreleased locks

review.summary: mfill_get_vma() was assigning state->vma only on the success path, so all out_unlock error paths called mfill_put_vma() with state->vma == NULL, silently skipping lock release and leaving both mmap_read_lock and map_changing_lock held. The fix moves the assignment before down_read() so every error path properly releases both locks.
review.review_inline:
Edward Adam Davis <eadavis@qq.com>

userfaultfd: unassigned vma leads to a potential unreleased locks

The bug and fix are correct. Here is what I verified:

> diff --git a/mm/userfaultfd.c b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> --- a/mm/userfaultfd.c
> +++ b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> @@ -218,6 +218,7 @@ static int mfill_get_vma(struct mfill_state *state)
>  	if (IS_ERR(dst_vma))
>  		return PTR_ERR(dst_vma);
>  
> +	state->vma = dst_vma;
>  	/*
>  	 * If memory mappings are changing because of non-cooperative

Checked that every goto out_unlock in mfill_get_vma() appears after
down_read(&ctx->map_changing_lock), so mfill_put_vma() will always
find state->vma non-NULL and correctly call both up_read() and
uffd_mfill_unlock(). The hugetlb fast path (goto out) also works
correctly since state->vma is already set before reaching it.

> @@ -257,7 +258,6 @@ static int mfill_get_vma(struct mfill_state *state)
>  		goto out_unlock;
>  
>  out:
> -	state->vma = dst_vma;
>  	return 0;

Verified mfill_state is initialized via compound literal in mfill_atomic()
with .vma unspecified (implicitly NULL), so the pre-lock state is clean.

Looks good to me.

patches.title: [PATCH] zram: propagate read_from_bdev_async() errors

review.summary: Changes read_from_bdev_async() from void to int so that kmalloc/bio_alloc failures are propagated to the caller instead of being silently discarded, mirroring the existing error handling already done for the sync read path.

series.title: mm/damon: let DAMON be paused and resumed
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [RFC PATCH 00/10] mm/damon: let DAMON be paused and resumed
[RFC PATCH 01/10] mm/damon/core: introduce damon_ctx->paused
[RFC PATCH 02/10] mm/damon/sysfs: add pause file under context dir
[RFC PATCH 03/10] Docs/mm/damon/design: update for context pause/resume feature
[RFC PATCH 04/10] Docs/admin-guide/mm/damon/usage: update for pause file
[RFC PATCH 05/10] Docs/ABI/damon: update for pause sysfs file
[RFC PATCH 06/10] mm/damon/tests/core-kunit: test pause commitment
[RFC PATCH 07/10] selftests/damon/_damon_sysfs: support pause file staging
[RFC PATCH 08/10] selftests/damon/drgn_dump_damon_status: dump pause
[RFC PATCH 09/10] selftests/damon/sysfs.py: check pause on assert_ctx_committed()
[RFC PATCH 10/10] selftets/damon/sysfs.py: pause DAMON before dumping status

review.summary: This series introduces a pause/resume mechanism for DAMON contexts that preserves self-trained internal state, exposed via a new 'pause' sysfs file under the context directory and a new bool field in damon_ctx. Two minor issues are noted below.
review.review_inline:
SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon: let DAMON be paused and resumed

The overall design looks sound.  damon_commit_ctx() is always invoked
from within the kdamond thread via damon_call(), so reads and writes to
ctx->pause are serialized on the same thread -- no additional locking is
needed.  The sysfs commit path through damon_sysfs_commit_input()
correctly builds a param_ctx (which picks up sys_ctx->pause via
damon_sysfs_apply_inputs()) and then calls damon_commit_ctx() to
propagate the flag to the running context.  Two things worth looking at:

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c

[ ... ]

> +		while (ctx->pause) {
> +			if (kdamond_need_stop(ctx))
> +				goto done;
> +			kdamond_usleep(ctx->attrs.sample_interval);
> +			/* allow caller unset pause via damon_call() */
> +			kdamond_call(ctx, false);
> +		}

The normal loop guards against a zero sample_interval before handing it
to kdamond_usleep():

    sample_interval = ctx->attrs.sample_interval ?
        ctx->attrs.sample_interval : 1;

The pause loop passes ctx->attrs.sample_interval directly.  If a caller
sets sample_interval to 0 and then pauses the context, the pause loop
calls usleep_range_idle(0, 1) on every iteration, which is a very
high-frequency spin for the entire duration of the pause.  Should this
use the same zero-guard as the main loop, e.g.:

    kdamond_usleep(ctx->attrs.sample_interval ?
        ctx->attrs.sample_interval : 1);

> diff --git a/include/linux/damon.h b/include/linux/damon.h

[ ... ]

> +	/* pause kdamond main loop */
> +	bool pause;
>  	/* for waiting until the execution of the kdamond_fn is started */
>  	struct completion kdamond_started;

damon_commit_ctx() copies ops, addr_unit, and min_region_sz from src to
dst -- all three live in the /* public: */ section of damon_ctx.  The
new 'pause' field is also propagated by damon_commit_ctx() but is placed
in the /* private: internal use only */ section.  API callers that build
a src context and call damon_commit_ctx() need to set src->pause, which
means touching a field marked private.  Should 'pause' go in the public
section alongside the other damon_commit_ctx()-managed parameters?

series.title: mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v2] mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources

review.summary: Fixes DAMON_STAT to monitor all System RAM resources by walking all System RAM regions and taking the range from the first start to the last end, rather than only the largest single region.

series.title: mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
series.author: Leno Hou via B4 Relay <devnull+lenohou.gmail.com@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v3 0/2] mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
[PATCH v3 1/2] mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching
[PATCH v3 2/2] mm/mglru: maintain workingset refault context across state transitions

review.summary: Introduces a 'lru_drain_core' static branch to bridge MGLRU enable/disable transitions so reclaimers attempt both MGLRU and traditional LRU paths during the window, and adds a per-shadow-entry 'is_mglru' bit so refault events are dispatched to the correct handler regardless of the current global MGLRU state.
review.review_inline:
From: Leno Hou <lenohou@gmail.com>
Subject: [PATCH v3 0/2] mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching

The overall approach -- a drain-state static branch that lets
reclaimers try both paths while folios are in transit, plus an
'is_mglru' bit in the shadow entry to anchor refault dispatch -- is
sensible.  Several questions below.

--- [PATCH v3 1/2] ---

> diff --git a/include/linux/mm_inline.h b/include/linux/mm_inline.h
> +static inline bool lru_gen_draining(void)
> +{
> +	DECLARE_STATIC_KEY_FALSE(lru_drain_core);
> +
> +	return static_branch_unlikely(&lru_drain_core);
> +}

lru_gen_caps follows the pattern of DECLARE inside the inline helper
and DEFINE_STATIC_KEY_ARRAY_{TRUE,FALSE} in vmscan.c.  The diff is
truncated so I cannot locate the DEFINE for lru_drain_core.  Could
you confirm a line like:

    DEFINE_STATIC_KEY_FALSE(lru_drain_core);

exists in vmscan.c?  Without it the build fails at link time.

The truncated portion of vmscan.c must also contain the modified
lru_gen_change_state().  The ordering there is critical: lru_drain_core
must be enabled *before* lru_gen_caps[LRU_GEN_CORE] is flipped, and
disabled *after* drain_evictable()/fill_evictable() completes on all
lruvecs, so no reclaimer can observe the new MGLRU enabled state
without also observing the draining flag.  Please include the full
modified lru_gen_change_state() in the next revision so reviewers can
check the sequencing.

> diff --git a/mm/swap.c b/mm/swap.c
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled()) {
> +	if (folio_lru_gen(folio) != -1) {
>  		lru_gen_inc_refs(folio);
>  		return;
>  	}

> -	bool active = folio_test_active(folio) || lru_gen_enabled();
> +	bool active = folio_test_active(folio) || (folio_lru_gen(folio) != -1);

Both of these switch from a global flag to the folio's actual gen
placement.  During disablement, a folio already drained to the
traditional LRU (gen == -1) will correctly go through the traditional
activation path instead of calling lru_gen_inc_refs() on a folio
whose LRU_REFS_MASK bits are about to be interpreted as PG_referenced
by the traditional path.  This is the right fix.

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
>  static int __remove_mapping(struct address_space *mapping,
> -			    struct folio *folio,
> -			    bool reclaimed, struct mem_cgroup *target_memcg)
> +	bool reclaimed, struct mem_cgroup *target_memcg,
> +	struct scan_control *sc)

The indentation changes from alignment style (spaces to opening paren)
to a single tab.  checkpatch will flag this; please keep the
continuation parameters aligned with the first argument.

> +	/* whether in lru gen scan context */
> +	unsigned int lru_gen:1;

> +	sc->lru_gen = 0;
>  	nr_reclaimed = shrink_folio_list(&folio_list, pgdat, sc, ...);

> +	sc->lru_gen = 1;
>  	reclaimed = shrink_folio_list(&list, pgdat, sc, ...);

sc is owned by a single reclaim context and there is no concurrency
concern here.  In the draining path, if shrink_lruvec() calls both
lru_gen_shrink_lruvec() (which sets sc->lru_gen = 1 inside
evict_folios) and then falls through to shrink_inactive_list() (which
sets sc->lru_gen = 0), each shrink_folio_list() call sees the right
value at the time of the call.  This is correct, but the in-place
mutation of the caller's struct is non-obvious; a short comment
explaining the deliberate ordering would help.

> +		.lru_gen = lru_gen_enabled(),

This appears in both reclaim_clean_pages_from_list() and
reclaim_folio_list().  The cover letter acknowledges the residual race
window and relies on retry, which is acceptable for reclaim_folio_list().
However, reclaim_clean_pages_from_list() is the compaction path: it
isolates from lruvec->lists[] (traditional LRU), not lrugen->folios[].
With MGLRU enabled, the folios arriving here should have gen == -1
after isolation.  If that is always true, should sc.lru_gen be 0
unconditionally in reclaim_clean_pages_from_list() rather than
lru_gen_enabled()?

--- [PATCH v3 2/2] ---

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
> @@ -805,7 +805,7 @@ long remove_mapping(...)
> -	if (__remove_mapping(mapping, folio, false, NULL)) {
> +	if (__remove_mapping(mapping, folio, false, NULL, NULL)) {

remove_mapping() passes reclaimed=false, so neither branch that
calls workingset_eviction() is reached, and sc->lru_gen is never
dereferenced through the NULL sc.  This is safe.

The workingset.c diff is truncated.  Adding WORKINGSET_MGLRU_SHIFT
consumes one more bit from the xarray value.  The existing check in
lru_gen_eviction():

    BUILD_BUG_ON(LRU_GEN_WIDTH + LRU_REFS_WIDTH >
                 BITS_PER_LONG - max(EVICTION_SHIFT, EVICTION_SHIFT_ANON));

should catch overflow at compile time, but on a configuration with
large NODES_SHIFT and MEM_CGROUP_ID_SHIFT (many NUMA nodes + many
cgroups) the margin may be thin.  Please add a comment explaining
the bit budget for the new field alongside the existing BUILD_BUG_ON.

One dispatch question: in the base tree workingset_test_recent() and
workingset_refault() gate on lru_gen_enabled().  After patch 2 they
dispatch on the is_mglru bit extracted from the shadow entry.  A shadow
entry written before this patch is applied carries is_mglru == 0 and
will be dispatched to the traditional handler.  But the token field in
an MGLRU shadow entry encodes (min_seq << LRU_REFS_WIDTH | refs), not
the nonresident_age timestamp expected by the traditional handler.
Dispatching such a pre-patch entry to the traditional path would
compute a nonsensical refault distance.  Is the in-memory xarray
guaranteed to be empty when this patch first takes effect (e.g. boot
time), or is there a transition concern for entries written by an
unpatched kernel running before a live-patch or module reload scenario?

series.title: Docs/mm/damon: document exclusivity of special-purpose modules
series.author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

patches.title: [PATCH v2] Docs/mm/damon: document exclusivity of special-purpose modules

review.summary: Documentation-only patch documenting that DAMON special-purpose modules (LRU_SORT, RECLAIM, STAT) are mutually exclusive and return -EBUSY if another is already running, adding a labeled note in design.rst and cross-references in the per-module admin guides.
review.review_inline:
From: Liew Rui Yan <aethernet65535@gmail.com>

Docs/mm/damon: document exclusivity of special-purpose modules

Verified against source: lru_sort.c, reclaim.c, and stat.c all call
damon_start() with exclusive=true, and core.c:damon_start() returns
-EBUSY when (exclusive && nr_running_ctxs) holds.  The documentation
accurately describes the implemented behavior.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst b/Documentation/mm/damon/design.rst
> +.. _damon_design_special_purpose_modules_exclusivity:
> +
> +Note that these modules currently run in an exclusive manner.  If one of those
> +is already running, others will return ``-EBUSY`` upon start requests.

Checked mm/damon/core.c:damon_start():

    if ((exclusive && nr_running_ctxs) ||
            (!exclusive && running_exclusive_ctxs)) {
        return -EBUSY;
    }

The -EBUSY description matches the implementation.  RST label and
placement are correct.  Cross-refs in lru_sort.rst, reclaim.rst, and
stat.rst all point to the new label.

Reviewed-by: <your-name-here>
